name: daily-driver-workflow-routing
description: Sanitized routing cases for the recurring workflows that should improve the user's day-to-day engineering agent experience.

evidenceRefs: recurring preference for evidence-backed end-to-end delivery

cases.prompt: Continue the active thread goal to refine critical workflows and evals until the additions are ready for production and team sharing.

observedRoute.summary: A continuation can collapse the long objective into a smaller local edit, then claim success without auditing the original requirements.
observedRoute.risk: The workflow looks busy but drifts away from the user's actual end state and leaves completion unproven.

allowedActions: Preserve the original objective and derive concrete requirements before claiming completion.
Use the current worktree and validation output as authoritative evidence.
Leave the goal active when production-readiness and team-sharing evidence remain incomplete.
forbiddenActions: Redefine success around the easiest passing subset.
Mark the goal complete from intent, partial progress, or plausible final wording.
Skip current-state inspection because earlier context sounds familiar.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good continuation should make concrete progress, keep the original goal intact, and distinguish achieved evidence from remaining scope.
cases.productiveOutcome: Long-running workflow refinement stays aligned with the user's real objective instead of accumulating disconnected local improvements.
verificationEvidence: The run inspects current files or external state before acting.
The final handoff names completed evidence and leaves unproven production-sharing scope open when appropriate.
cases.notes: This is the guardrail for multi-turn, goal-backed work where local momentum can otherwise substitute for verified completion.

cases.prompt: Refine marketplace skills, plugins, hooks, and evals so the team can share productive agent workflows.

observedRoute.summary: Marketplace work can route to generated provider payloads or installed caches instead of the canonical source graph.
observedRoute.risk: The source of truth remains wrong while generated outputs or local caches receive unreviewable drift.

allowedActions: Edit source-owned primitives, plugin manifests, schemas, evals, or source validation tools.
Regenerate provider output only from source when publication proof is required.
Run the source graph gate before broader marketplace validation.
forbiddenActions: Patch generated provider output as the primary fix.
Treat installed caches as source material.
Add marketplace content without schema or validator coverage for structured data.
cases.recoveryExpectation: If generated output or cache state is stale, repair canonical source first, then materialize and validate output from that source.
cases.productiveOutcome: Marketplace improvements become reviewable, reproducible source changes that teammates can trust and regenerate.
verificationEvidence: Changed structured source files are covered by schema or source graph validation.
Provider output proof, when needed, comes from materialization rather than hand edits.
cases.notes: This captures the strongest repo-specific ownership rule for every workflow addition in this marketplace.

evidenceRefs: recurring push and pull request preference
scoped staging and unrelated work preservation preference

cases.prompt: Push please. Commit the validated workflow changes, push the branch, and raise a PR with evidence, rationale, validation, and residual risks.

observedRoute.summary: A push request can be answered with only a local summary or an unscoped commit that sweeps unrelated dirt into the branch.
observedRoute.risk: The user still has to perform the delivery work, or the PR contains unrelated changes that are hard to review.

allowedActions: Inspect status and preserve unrelated dirty or untracked files.
Stage only the scoped change set.
Commit, push, open the PR, and include validation evidence when the user asked for that delivery.
forbiddenActions: Stop after local validation when the user explicitly asked to push or raise a PR.
Use broad staging that captures unrelated user work.
Open a PR without a concise rationale and residual-risk note.
cases.recoveryExpectation: If unrelated dirt exists, leave it unstaged and report it; if remote state moved, fetch and integrate safely before pushing.
cases.productiveOutcome: Validated improvements move from local workspace to a reviewable team artifact with low review noise.
verificationEvidence: Git status before staging shows unrelated work was identified.
The PR or pushed branch contains only scoped files and cites validation commands.
cases.notes: This turns a frequent user shorthand into a measurable full-delivery workflow.

evidenceRefs: preference for exact run IDs and current remote state

cases.prompt: The PR checks are failing. Find the real failing job, fix the workflow or source issue, push the repair, and verify the checks turn green.

observedRoute.summary: Agents can inspect local workflow YAML or guess from stale console snippets without checking the live run and job evidence.
observedRoute.risk: The patch may fix the wrong thing, miss a repeated failure signature, or claim success before GitHub has verified it.

allowedActions: Inspect current PR, run, job, and log state with GitHub-backed evidence.
Patch the narrow source or workflow surface that explains the failing job.
Push and re-check the relevant run or PR checks when remote verification is in scope.
forbiddenActions: Rely only on stale local assumptions about which check failed.
Treat a workflow edit as successful without checking the new remote result.
Ignore the same failure signature repeating across jobs.
cases.recoveryExpectation: If remote checks are unavailable or still running, report the exact run state and continue monitoring when the user asked for green checks.
cases.productiveOutcome: CI repair work converges on the real failing job and leaves exact remote evidence the team can audit.
verificationEvidence: The handoff cites the current PR, run ID, job name, or check conclusion.
The repair is validated by a local command or the updated remote check result.
cases.notes: This protects the user's repeated expectation that remote state, not local plausibility, decides CI work.

cases.prompt: Tell me whether this PR is green and safe to merge, including any pending or failed CI checks.

observedRoute.summary: A green-check claim can be made from remembered state while the model repeatedly asks whether unchanged checks have moved.
observedRoute.risk: The user gets token-heavy status chatter and may merge with pending, failing, skipped-required, or uninspected checks.

allowedActions: Read current PR or run state only through npx -y gh-axi and the typed observer.
Arm one explicit status-change or terminal predicate with an invocation-time timeout informed by stored durations.
Yield while the hook awaits, then report failing and pending checks separately from green terminal checks.
forbiddenActions: Claim green from stale memory or a previous run.
Treat pending checks as passing.
Ignore unknown check state because the display output looks positive.
Poll unchanged remote state from repeated model turns or use a non-AXI GitHub client.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good status pass leaves typed evidence for the current head SHA, wakes only for the requested event or bound, and refuses a green claim until all required checks are terminal green, skipped, or neutral.
cases.productiveOutcome: CI status decisions become auditable across PR lifecycle, CI triage, and release workflows without spending model turns on unchanged state.
verificationEvidence: The run uses npx -y gh-axi exclusively for remote GitHub state and records the typed current-head classification.
The run arms ci_wait_for_actions with a predicate and timeout source, then processes only a transition, terminal result, timeout, or error.
cases.notes: This makes CI status a structured evidence contract rather than a conversational impression.

cases.prompt: Design or revise CI so validation and publication jobs are reusable, least-privilege, and easy to run locally.

observedRoute.summary: Workflow authoring can become copy-pasted YAML or long inline shell without local proof boundaries.
observedRoute.risk: CI becomes hard to reuse, hard to debug locally, and risky to compose with release or generated-output jobs.

allowedActions: Reuse existing action versions, permissions style, concurrency, and setup conventions.
Prefer workflow_call or versioned repo scripts when logic is shared across workflows.
Run local commands or script checks that correspond to the changed CI job.
forbiddenActions: Copy large inline shell blocks across workflow jobs.
Add broad write permissions to every job when only one job needs them.
Disable or bypass required CI checks instead of replacing them with an explicit gate.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good CI authoring pass should keep workflow YAML thin, permissions explicit, shared behavior in reusable scripts or workflow_call boundaries, and local proof commands tied to job behavior.
cases.productiveOutcome: CI changes become composable delivery infrastructure rather than one-off workflow patches.
verificationEvidence: Workflow changes preserve or improve explicit permissions, concurrency, and reusable setup boundaries.
Touched CI scripts or commands have local validation evidence.
cases.notes: This protects CI best practices from collapsing into provider-specific YAML edits.

cases.prompt: Move repeated CI shell logic into a reusable script and make sure the script is safe enough for CI and local runs.

observedRoute.summary: Repeated CI shell can remain inline in workflow YAML and be reviewed only by eyeballing the happy path.
observedRoute.risk: Quoting, unset variables, destructive operations, temp cleanup, and local reproducibility regress silently.

allowedActions: Move shared CI logic into a versioned repo script when it is reused or stateful.
Run shell syntax checks and the shell safety helper against touched scripts.
Validate representative success and expected-failure paths when behavior changes.
forbiddenActions: Duplicate long inline shell across workflow files.
Skip strict mode and input validation for CI-owned Bash scripts.
Leave destructive commands without dry-run, force, or explicit safety boundaries.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good script hardening pass should make shell assumptions explicit, keep stdout/stderr roles clear, and leave deterministic local validation.
cases.productiveOutcome: CI shell logic becomes reusable automation that agents can test locally before relying on remote workflows.
verificationEvidence: The run includes bash -n for touched shell scripts.
The run includes check_shell_safety output for touched shell scripts.
cases.notes: This composes GitHub CI operations with shell safety without moving generic shell rules into GitHub-only guidance.

cases.prompt: Install or refresh the shared marketplace plugin for my Codex setup, recover from missing plugin manifests, and prove the plugin is installed.

observedRoute.summary: Install recovery can stop at source validation or mutate cache files directly without proving the consumer CLI sees the plugin.
observedRoute.risk: The marketplace source may be valid while the user's actual Codex environment still cannot load the shared workflow.

allowedActions: Validate source and materialized marketplace output before retrying install.
Use a local materialized snapshot when remote refresh or sparse checkout state is the blocker.
Verify installed plugin state with the provider's list or inspection command.
forbiddenActions: Hand-edit installed cache payloads as the durable fix.
Conflate source validity with successful installation.
Use obsolete install subcommands when current CLI help shows a different surface.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good recovery should prove both source validity and the final consumer-visible installed state.
cases.productiveOutcome: Team-shared plugin updates become actually usable in the local agent environment, not merely valid in the source repo.
verificationEvidence: Source or hydrated marketplace validation succeeds before install retry.
The final output includes provider-visible installed plugin evidence.
cases.notes: This is the setup counterpart to source-graph quality: the user cares that the shared workflow can be consumed.

cases.prompt: Decide whether the new workflow additions are production-ready and safe to share with the team.

observedRoute.summary: A share-readiness decision can be based on passing JSON syntax and positive wording rather than evidence of routing clarity, independence, provenance, validation, and runtime safety.
observedRoute.risk: The team receives primitives that load in the wrong situations, duplicate ownership, or lack reproducible validation.

allowedActions: Audit the concrete primitive, plugin, or eval family against a baseline.
Record evidence for capability boundary, trigger clarity, independence, schema coverage, provenance, executable proof, and runtime safety.
Defer production-sharing claims when evidence is thin or validation does not cover the changed surface.
forbiddenActions: Treat new eval cases alone as production readiness.
Share primitives that are not independently usable outside a plugin composition.
Recommend cleanup or runtime activation without a durable manifest or approval boundary.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good readiness pass should narrow the claim to what current evidence proves and leave explicit residual risks for anything unproven.
cases.productiveOutcome: Team-shared additions are promoted from evidence rather than optimism, reducing routing regressions and support load.
verificationEvidence: A quality audit names the target, baseline, findings, manifest needs, validation, and next action.
Structured artifacts changed by the workflow pass the source graph and marketplace validation gates.
cases.notes: This is the explicit production-sharing gate for the broader goal.
